IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of constructing semantic hierarchies and reports substantial empirical gains: MFusion achieves 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1 on a manual test set, outperforming multiple baselines, and combining MFusion with Wikipedia/CilinE boosts F1 to 82.01%. Its fusion of a generative MLP, discriminative Elman RNN, and a lexical rule — together with a language-independent claim — is likely to influence how researchers combine generative and discriminative signals for hypernym extraction. Given reported limitations (proxy targets from the generative model and failures when embeddings are very similar), the contribution is strong and practically useful but not fully transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles the important task of building semantic hierarchies and proposes a novel fusion architecture (generative MLP + discriminative Elman RNN + lexical rule) that attains strong empirical gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1) and outperforms listed baselines. Combining MFusion with manual hierarchies (Wikipedia and CilinE) further improves F1 to 82.01%, demonstrating practical utility. The fusion approach and the claim of language-independence open promising research directions for hybrid models, although reported failure modes when embeddings are very similar may constrain some applications.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of constructing semantic hierarchies and reports substantial empirical gains: MFusion achieves 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1 on a manual test set, outperforming multiple baselines, and combining MFusion with Wikipedia/CilinE boosts F1 to 82.01%. Its fusion of a generative MLP, discriminative Elman RNN, and a lexical rule — together with a language-independent claim — is likely to influence how researchers combine generative and discriminative signals for hypernym extraction. Given reported limitations (proxy targets from the generative model and failures when embeddings are very similar), the contribution is strong and practically useful but not fully transformative.

Combining MFusion with manual hierarchies (Wikipedia and CilinE) further improves F1 to 82.01%, demonstrating practical utility. The fusion approach and the claim of language-independence open promising research directions for hybrid models, although reported failure modes when embeddings are very similar may constrain some applications.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from methodology (MLP + RNN + lexical rule over Skip-gram embeddings) to experiments (datasets, baselines, metrics) and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and related work is covered thoroughly; notation and hyperparameters are reported and thus seem consistent and well-defined. Figures/tables are informative insofar as precise results (precision, recall, F1) and many baselines are reported, and the writing quality is adequate though a few explanations (e.g., the generative model producing a proxy/fake target) could be made slightly clearer. | B(4): The paper is well organized (clear methodology, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics presented) and the main contributions are stated early (MFusion combining MLP, RNN and lexical rule with reported results such as 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1, and improvement to 82.01% F1 when combined with Wikipedia/CilinE). Notation and presentation appear mostly consistent and the experimental tables and comparisons are informative. A few minor clarity issues remain (e.g., the description of the generative model producing a "proxy" target and some failure-case explanations could be more explicit), but overall the writing is adequate.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are strong in comparing MFusion against a wide set of baselines (pattern-, distributional-, and embedding-based) and report clear metrics (P/R/F1) including improvements when combining with Wikipedia and CilinE; the paper also provides component-level results (MMLPgen., MRNNdis., MMLP+RNN) so ablation of main modules is present. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and limitations/failure cases are explicitly discussed (e.g., failures when hyponym/hypernym embeddings are very similar). Reproducibility is partially supported by reported hyperparameters and dataset descriptions (Baidubaike, Fu et al. test set), but code is not mentioned and the claimed language-independence was not empirically validated beyond the Chinese corpus, representing a modest gap. | B(4): The experiments are strong and compare MFusion against multiple baselines with concrete metrics (e.g., 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1) and show improvements when combined with manual hierarchies, and the paper presents component-level comparisons (MMLPgen, MRNNdis, MMLP+RNN) that serve as ablations. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and limitations/failure cases are explicitly discussed (e.g., failures when hyponym–hypernym embeddings are very similar and the generative proxy issue). Reproducibility is partly supported (embeddings source, datasets, and hyperparameters reported) but code is not mentioned, leaving some practical reproduction details missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are strong in comparing MFusion against a wide set of baselines (pattern-, distributional-, and embedding-based) and report clear metrics (P/R/F1) including improvements when combining with Wikipedia and CilinE; the paper also provides component-level results (MMLPgen., MRNNdis., MMLP+RNN) so ablation of main modules is present. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and limitations/failure cases are explicitly discussed (e.g., failures when hyponym/hypernym embeddings are very similar). Reproducibility is partially supported by reported hyperparameters and dataset descriptions (Baidubaike, Fu et al. test set), but code is not mentioned and the claimed language-independence was not empirically validated beyond the Chinese corpus, representing a modest gap.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong and compare MFusion against multiple baselines with concrete metrics (e.g., 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1) and show improvements when combined with manual hierarchies, and the paper presents component-level comparisons (MMLPgen, MRNNdis, MMLP+RNN) that serve as ablations. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and limitations/failure cases are explicitly discussed (e.g., failures when hyponym–hypernym embeddings are very similar and the generative proxy issue). Reproducibility is partly supported (embeddings source, datasets, and hyperparameters reported) but code is not mentioned, leaving some practical reproduction details missing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main empirical claims are supported by experiments: MFusion is evaluated against appropriate baselines (including embedding-based MEmb) on the same manually labeled test set and reports clear metrics (91.60% P, 74.20% F1) using large Baidubaike-trained embeddings, and hyperparameters/baselines are documented. There is no indication of mathematical derivation errors in the summary, but formal proofs/derivations are not described, and no statistical significance testing of results is reported. A notable unsupported assumption is the blanket claim of language-independence (no cross-lingual experiments) and the justification for using a generative proxy target is acknowledged but not fully analyzed. Overall the experiments largely justify the conclusions, with minor methodological gaps (significance testing, cross-language validation) preventing a perfect score. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: MFusion outperforms the listed baselines (reported 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1 on the manually labeled test set) and combining with Wikipedia/CilinE increases F1 to 82.01%. The experimental methodology appears largely sound—appropriate baselines (pattern-, distributional-, and embedding-based methods), a sizable Baidubaike-trained skip‑gram embedding set, and standard P/R/F1 metrics are used and hyperparameters are reported. However, the generative MLP’s use of a proxy/fake target is acknowledged as heuristic and lacks theoretical justification, the paper claims language‑independence despite evaluation on a single Chinese corpus, and no statistical significance tests are reported; these are minor but notable gaps that do not invalidate the presented results.

reviewer_a.justification: The main empirical claims are supported by experiments: MFusion is evaluated against appropriate baselines (including embedding-based MEmb) on the same manually labeled test set and reports clear metrics (91.60% P, 74.20% F1) using large Baidubaike-trained embeddings, and hyperparameters/baselines are documented. There is no indication of mathematical derivation errors in the summary, but formal proofs/derivations are not described, and no statistical significance testing of results is reported. A notable unsupported assumption is the blanket claim of language-independence (no cross-lingual experiments) and the justification for using a generative proxy target is acknowledged but not fully analyzed. Overall the experiments largely justify the conclusions, with minor methodological gaps (significance testing, cross-language validation) preventing a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: MFusion outperforms the listed baselines (reported 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1 on the manually labeled test set) and combining with Wikipedia/CilinE increases F1 to 82.01%. The experimental methodology appears largely sound—appropriate baselines (pattern-, distributional-, and embedding-based methods), a sizable Baidubaike-trained skip‑gram embedding set, and standard P/R/F1 metrics are used and hyperparameters are reported. However, the generative MLP’s use of a proxy/fake target is acknowledged as heuristic and lacks theoretical justification, the paper claims language‑independence despite evaluation on a single Chinese corpus, and no statistical significance tests are reported; these are minor but notable gaps that do not invalidate the presented results.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a fusion learning architecture (MFusion) that integrates a generative MLP, a discriminative Elman RNN, and a lexical-structure rule to construct semantic hierarchies—this specific combination and application to embedding-based hypernym extraction is a non-trivial, practical extension of existing approaches. However, the underlying components (MLP generators, RNN discriminators, lexical rules) and the problem of hypernym/hyponym extraction are well-studied, and the authors themselves note limitations of direct generative hypernym production (using a proxy instead), which reduces conceptual novelty. The work is differentiated from prior embedding baselines (e.g., MEmb) and shows strong empirical gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1), but overall it reads as an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. | B(3): The paper's MFusion architecture (combining a generative MLP that produces proxy hypernym vectors, a discriminative Elman RNN classifier, and a lexical-structure rule) is a novel composition of components but the individual elements (MLP/RNN over embeddings, lexical rules) and the task (hypernym/hierarchy construction) are well-established. The authors clearly differentiate contributions via component ablations and comparisons to multiple baselines (e.g., MEmb, pattern-based methods), showing empirical gains. However, the approach reads as a non-trivial but largely incremental integration of known techniques rather than a fundamentally new formulation, so it is informative but not highly surprising to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a fusion learning architecture (MFusion) that integrates a generative MLP, a discriminative Elman RNN, and a lexical-structure rule to construct semantic hierarchies—this specific combination and application to embedding-based hypernym extraction is a non-trivial, practical extension of existing approaches. However, the underlying components (MLP generators, RNN discriminators, lexical rules) and the problem of hypernym/hyponym extraction are well-studied, and the authors themselves note limitations of direct generative hypernym production (using a proxy instead), which reduces conceptual novelty. The work is differentiated from prior embedding baselines (e.g., MEmb) and shows strong empirical gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1), but overall it reads as an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's MFusion architecture (combining a generative MLP that produces proxy hypernym vectors, a discriminative Elman RNN classifier, and a lexical-structure rule) is a novel composition of components but the individual elements (MLP/RNN over embeddings, lexical rules) and the task (hypernym/hierarchy construction) are well-established. The authors clearly differentiate contributions via component ablations and comparisons to multiple baselines (e.g., MEmb, pattern-based methods), showing empirical gains. However, the approach reads as a non-trivial but largely incremental integration of known techniques rather than a fundamentally new formulation, so it is informative but not highly surprising to experts.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares MFusion to a broad set of appropriate baselines (pattern-based MPattern, Snow, distributional/invCL, embedding-based MEmb, and several others) and reports standard metrics (P/R/F1) on the same manually labeled test set used by Fu et al. (2014). It also includes component-level comparisons/ablations (MMLPgen, MRNNdis, MMLP+RNN) and claims thorough related-work coverage. However, the summary does not confirm that all baselines were evaluated under identical training conditions (e.g., same embeddings/data) and the paper does not appear to acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive on fairness/limitations. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines (pattern-based MPattern/Hearst, Snow et al., balAPinc, invCL, Fu et al. 2013 MWeb, an embedding baseline MEmb, plus lexical-rule and component ablations). It evaluates on the same manually labeled test set used by Fu et al. (2014) using standard P/R/F1 metrics and reports component-level results (MMLPgen, MRNNdis, MMLP+RNN), which supports fair and informative comparisons. The authors claim and show that MFusion outperforms listed baselines and report limitations; however the summary does not confirm whether all baselines were re-run under identical embedding/training conditions, so a minor uncertainty remains.

(2014). It also includes component-level comparisons/ablations (MMLPgen, MRNNdis, MMLP+RNN) and claims thorough related-work coverage. However, the summary does not confirm that all baselines were evaluated under identical training conditions (e.g., same embeddings/data) and the paper does not appear to acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive on fairness/limitations.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML task (semantic hierarchy / hypernym construction) and proposes MFusion (generative MLP + discriminative Elman RNN + lexical rule) operating over Skip-gram embeddings trained on Baidubaike, which is squarely within top-conference scope. It uses community-expected methodology and evaluation (precision/recall/F1 on a manually labeled test set used by Fu et al., 2014) and compares against a broad set of established baselines (pattern-, embedding-, and lexical-based), additionally reporting gains when combined with manual hierarchies (Wikipedia, CilinE). The work is well-positioned for the venue, though the claim of language-independence is only empirically supported with Chinese resources, a minor concern for generalization. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (automatic construction of semantic hierarchies/hypernym extraction) using neural models (a generative MLP and discriminative Elman RNN) over word embeddings, which is squarely within top-tier venue scope. The methodology and empirical setup (Skip-gram embeddings from Baidubaike, comparison to prior pattern/embedding baselines, use of precision/recall/F1 and a publicly referenced test set from Fu et al. 2014) match community expectations. The work is positioned with thorough related work coverage and shows improvements over multiple baselines, making it relevant to the NLP/ML research audience.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/ML task (semantic hierarchy / hypernym construction) and proposes MFusion (generative MLP + discriminative Elman RNN + lexical rule) operating over Skip-gram embeddings trained on Baidubaike, which is squarely within top-conference scope. It uses community-expected methodology and evaluation (precision/recall/F1 on a manually labeled test set used by Fu et al., 2014) and compares against a broad set of established baselines (pattern-, embedding-, and lexical-based), additionally reporting gains when combined with manual hierarchies (Wikipedia, CilinE). The work is well-positioned for the venue, though the claim of language-independence is only empirically supported with Chinese resources, a minor concern for generalization.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (automatic construction of semantic hierarchies/hypernym extraction) using neural models (a generative MLP and discriminative Elman RNN) over word embeddings, which is squarely within top-tier venue scope. The methodology and empirical setup (Skip-gram embeddings from Baidubaike, comparison to prior pattern/embedding baselines, use of precision/recall/F1 and a publicly referenced test set from Fu et al. 2014) match community expectations. The work is positioned with thorough related work coverage and shows improvements over multiple baselines, making it relevant to the NLP/ML research audience.

main_claims: Proposes a fusion learning architecture (MFusion) that combines a generative MLP, a discriminative Elman RNN, and a simple lexical-structure rule to construct semantic hierarchies from word embeddings.
MFusion achieves 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1 on a manually labeled test dataset, outperforming listed prior methods (including an embedding-based baseline MEmb).
Combining MFusion with manually-built hierarchies (Wikipedia and CilinE) further improves F1 to 82.01%.
The fusion learning architecture is stated to be language-independent.
_summary.methodology: Fuse a generative Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) that generates hypernym vectors and a discriminative Elman Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) that classifies hyponym–candidate-hypernym sequences, both operating over Skip-gram word embeddings trained on Baidubaike, with results assisted by a simple lexical-structure rule.
datasets: Baidubaike corpus (used to train embeddings; ~30M sentences, ~780M words; 0.56M word embeddings)
Manually labeled test dataset (same test dataset as Fu et al., 2014)
Wikipedia (used as a manually-built hierarchy for combination)
CilinE (used as a manually-built hierarchy for combination)
limitations: Fails on some hyponym–hypernym pairs when their embeddings are very similar and the method cannot find a suitable projection (example: relation between 乌头属(Aconitum) and 毛茛科(Ranunculaceae)).
Direct generation of hypernyms by generative architectures is stated to be usually impractical, so the generative model produces a proxy/fake target similar to the true hypernym.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic hierarchy construction / hypernym-hyponym extraction
